allergens: gluten (possible trace from fish cake processing)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker bowl of fishball soup with bee hoon weighs approximately 450g, comprising clear fish broth, 4–6 fishballs (~80g), fish cake slices (~30g), and cooked bee hoon (rice vermicelli, ~100g).

nutrient_source.title: Fishball Soup with Bee Hoon — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for fishball soup with bee hoon. All per_100g values reflect the composite dish as served (broth + fishballs + bee hoon). Confidence 1.0 for all macros and micronutrients as reported in input record from verified HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice vermicelli (bee hoon) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is derived from the GI of rice vermicelli (bee hoon), the primary carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Rice vermicelli GI ~58 is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. Fishballs and clear broth contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially affect the composite GI. Glycemic load per 450g bowl = (58 × 49.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 28.7. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 855mg per bowl (190mg/100g × 450g), representing approximately 43% of the 2,000mg daily sodium RDI; sodium rises to 1,050–1,250mg if soy sauce is added at the table.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit fishball soup to occasional consumption, request reduced fishballs and no fish cake to lower sodium by approximately 200mg per bowl, and strictly avoid adding soy sauce or chilli sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load of 28.7 per bowl is high, driven by approximately 100g cooked bee hoon (rice vermicelli) with GI ~58; the broth and fishballs contribute negligible glycemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a half-portion of bee hoon (approximately 50g cooked) to reduce the glycemic load to approximately 14.4, or substitute with a lower-GI noodle option such as glass noodles (GI ~26–35).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load of 855mg per bowl is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 0.3g/100g (1.35g per bowl) and trans fat is 0g, which are favourable; however, the processed fishballs and fish cake contribute significant dietary sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the low saturated fat and trans fat profile is favourable, but the high sodium content (855mg per bowl) warrants caution — patients should limit portion frequency to 2–3 times per week and avoid sodium-adding condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g); fishballs provide selenium (10mcg/100g), vitamin B12 (0.5mcg/100g), and complete fish protein; high sodium (855mg/bowl) is the primary concern for patients on NSAIDs.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fishball soup is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory fish protein, but sodium intake should be monitored carefully in patients taking NSAIDs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fishball soup with bee hoon is one of Singapore's most iconic and ubiquitous hawker dishes, served at virtually every hawker centre and food court across the island. The dish consists of a clear, lightly seasoned fish broth containing bouncy handmade or factory-produced fishballs (鱼丸), sliced fish cake, and bee hoon (rice vermicelli). Fishballs are a Teochew specialty, traditionally made by pounding fresh yellow tail or Spanish mackerel with salt until a springy, elastic paste forms, then shaping into smooth spheres. The broth is typically seasoned with white pepper, salt, and sometimes a small amount of sesame oil, and garnished with spring onion and fried shallots. The dish is perceived as a 'light and healthy' meal due to its clear broth and low fat content (1g fat per 100g; 4.5g per bowl), but this perception is clinically misleading: a standard 450g bowl delivers 855mg sodium — 43% of the 2,000mg daily RDI — primarily from the processed fishballs, fish cake, and broth. Adding soy sauce or chilli sauce at the table raises sodium to 1,050–1,250mg per bowl. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the bee hoon (rice vermicelli, GI ~58, medium) is the primary glycemic concern, contributing a glycemic load of approximately 28.7 per bowl. The dish provides 315 kcal per bowl, 18g protein, and useful amounts of selenium (45mcg), vitamin B12 (2.25mcg), and phosphorus (292.5mg) from the fish-based ingredients. Fishball soup is suitable for halal and dairy-free diets. The 'clear soup equals healthy' misconception is one of the most important nutritional myths to address in Singapore dietary counselling.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), the primary approved source for Singaporean hawker dishes, with confidence 1.0 reported for all macros and micronutrients. GI value of 58 for rice vermicelli is well-documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All multilingual names verified. Confidence rated high.

All core macros (protein_g 4.0, fat_g 1.0, carbohydrate_g 11.0) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore. Per-bowl values (450g): energy 315 kcal, protein 18g, fat 4.5g, carbohydrate 49.5g, sodium 855mg, glycemic load 28.7. The 'clear soup = healthy' misconception is clinically significant: sodium content of 855mg per bowl (43% daily RDI) is the primary nutritional concern despite the dish's light appearance.
